# Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method

The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.

## Vietnamese Telex Rules

### Diacritics

Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
Tones have a separate section.

#### Empty

- Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
- Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
  characters.

#### Circumflex

- Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.

#### Breve

- Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.

#### Horn

- Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.

#### Stroke

- Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.

#### Apply rules

- Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
- _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
  existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
  - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
  - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
- _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
  valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
  - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Override rules

- New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
  - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
- Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
- Keep tone untouched.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Cancel rules

- The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
  produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
- The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
  - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
  - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
- The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
- Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
  remains until the new word.
  - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Auto-fill rules

- The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
  character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
- Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
  `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
  - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
  - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
- The new input character is appended literally.
- Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.

### Tones

Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
These vowel could take any tone.

#### Level (ngang)

- The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
  corresponding variants.
- If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.

#### Rising (sắc)

- Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
  -> `Ớ`.

#### Falling (huyền)

- Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
  -> `Ờ`.

#### Dipping-rising (hỏi)

- Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
  `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.

#### Rising-glottalized (ngã)

- Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
  -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.

#### Falling-glottalized (nặng)

- Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
  -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.

#### Apply rules

- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
  position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
- If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.

#### Cancel rules

- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
  the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
- If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
  position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Reset rules

- Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
  change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
  any tone (level), append the new character literally.
- If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
  position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Override rules

- Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
  from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
  the word. The override tone is always successful.

#### Scan pseudo-word

- Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
  the tone in the correct position.
- The existing characters must not be empty.
- Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
- If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
  character to scan, then stop.
- If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
  backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
- After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
  `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
  `<consonant><vowels>`.

#### Tone position rules

The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:

- Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
  tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
- Exceptions:
  - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
- If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
- If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
  - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
  - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
    prioritize the first to the last in the list.
  - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
    `U` count as part of the consonant.
  - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
  - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
    consonants, put on second vowel.
  - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
- Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
- Could not put tone for other cases.

input.task: Review the diacritic-rule production paths in src/liblex.zig for memory-safety risks and missing invariants. Focus on State.add, append_literal, buffer_effective indexing, C ABI wrappers, and assert placement. Provide concrete findings and suggested assertions, not a rewrite.
input.context: Workspace is a Zig Vietnamese Telex engine. Diacritic rules mutate a fixed [16]Span buffer and use u8 buffer_length. Project production builds should use ReleaseSafe. Current `zig test src/liblex.zig` passes. I want a second opinion before finalizing a review report.

run.result:
1) **TL;DR**

I do **not** see an immediate out-of-bounds bug in the current diacritic-rule paths under the intended invariants and `ReleaseSafe`: writes to `buffer_effective` are guarded, and the `-1`/`-2` reads are mostly preceded by length checks or `literal_index` gating.  
The review report should, however, call out **four concrete invariant gaps**: missing `u8` overflow guard in `append_literal`, incomplete precondition in `buffer_effective_last`, missing “long buffer implies no more effective-buffer reads” invariant in `State.add`, and incomplete C ABI validation in `lex_add` / `lex_backspace` (plus `lex_state_init` not rejecting null explicitly).

2) **Recommended approach (simple path, S <1h)**

Report these findings and suggest adding these assertions only:

1. **`append_literal` needs an explicit `buffer_length` overflow guard**  
   File: `src/liblex.zig:731-747`

   High-confidence issue:
   - Comment says `buffer_length` resets after 255 (`src/liblex.zig:228-230`), but that reset is **not implemented**.
   - `append_literal` always does `self.buffer_length += 1;`.
   - In `ReleaseSafe`, this becomes a runtime trap after 255 inputs. That is not an OOB write, but it is still a production-path failure.

   Suggested assertion:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_length < std.math.maxInt(u8));
   ```
   Place it immediately before `self.buffer_length += 1`.

   Optional related invariant at entry:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len or self.mode == .literal or self.literal_index != null);
   ```

2. **`buffer_effective_last` should assert `buffer_length > 0` itself**  
   File: `src/liblex.zig:749-755`

   Current code only asserts:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len);
   ```
   but then indexes:
   ```zig
   self.buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 1]
   ```
   So the helper’s real contract is “`1 <= buffer_length <= len`”, not just “`buffer_length <= len`”.

   Suggested assertion:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_length > 0);
   assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len);
   ```

   This is important because it moves the safety contract into the helper that does the indexing, instead of relying entirely on callers.

3. **`State.add` is missing the converse long-buffer invariant**  
   File: `src/liblex.zig:249-272`

   Today `add` checks that `literal_index`, if present, is sane. What it does **not** assert is the safety-critical converse:

   > if `buffer_length` is already beyond `[16]Span`, then the state must be in a mode that guarantees we never read `buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 1]` or `-2`.

   Suggested assertion near the top of `add`:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len or self.mode == .literal or self.literal_index != null);
   ```

   Why this matters:
   - Current logic is safe because once Telex overflows the effective buffer, `literal_index` is set and subsequent calls short-circuit into literal append paths.
   - But that safety currently exists as an emergent property, not as an explicit invariant.
   - This assertion would catch future regressions or corrupted state before any `buffer_effective_last()` / `self.buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 2]` access.

4. **C ABI wrappers should explicitly reject null and consistently assert alignment**  
   Files: `src/liblex.zig:1665-1670`, `1718-1725`; header `src/liblex.h:17-25`

   Concrete findings:
   - `lex_state_init` checks alignment, but **null passes that check** because `0 % align == 0`.
   - `lex_add` and `lex_backspace` do **not** mirror the alignment assert at all.
   - All three wrappers then cast and dereference the pointer.

   Suggested assertions in **all** three wrappers:
   ```zig
   assert(@intFromPtr(state) != 0);
   assert(@intFromPtr(state) % @alignOf(State) == 0);
   ```

   Notes for the report:
   - This does not let you verify allocation **size** from a raw pointer; `lex_state_size`/header docs remain the only size contract.
   - But it does make the ABI contract much clearer and catches bad C callers earlier.

5. **Tighten `buffer_modification_index` to the live prefix, not just array bounds**  
   File: `src/liblex.zig:253-255`

   Current assert:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_modification_index == null or self.buffer_modification_index.? < self.buffer_effective.len);
   ```

   Better invariant:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_modification_index == null or
       (self.buffer_modification_index.? < self.buffer_effective.len and
        self.buffer_modification_index.? < self.buffer_length));
   ```

   This is more of a correctness / future-proofing guard than a current memory-safety bug, but it is worth mentioning because the field represents a position in the **live** buffer, not just the allocated array.

3) **Rationale and trade-offs**

- The current diacritic paths are **mostly disciplined**:
  - `append_literal` only writes when `buffer_length < 16`.
  - Reads at `buffer_length - 1` and `buffer_length - 2` are generally gated by `buffer_length == 0` / `< 2` checks or by `literal_index != null`.
  - That means I would **not** report an existing clear OOB read/write in the diacritic branches themselves.

- The real problem is that some of the safety assumptions are only encoded indirectly:
  - helper preconditions are incomplete,
  - long-input behavior depends on a non-obvious invariant,
  - and the C ABI assumes well-behaved callers more than the rest of the code does.

- I would keep the report focused on **assert strengthening**, not redesign. A rewrite is unnecessary right now.

4) **Risks and guardrails**

- **Biggest real production risk today:** long-running input eventually hits `u8` overflow and traps in `ReleaseSafe`.  
  That is an availability issue, not an OOB write.

- **FFI risk:** null / misaligned C pointers are not defended consistently.  
  In particular, `lex_state_init`’s current alignment assert does **not** reject null.

- **Guardrail on wording:** because you’re targeting `ReleaseSafe`, I’d avoid claiming “memory corruption” unless you verify a `ReleaseFast` build too. The safer wording is:
  - “no immediate OOB found under intended invariants,”
  - “but several invariants are missing or only implicit,”
  - “and one long-input trap path is real.”

5) **When to consider the advanced path**

Consider a more invasive hardening pass only if one of these becomes true:

- you want the C ABI to survive **hostile/untrusted callers** instead of trapping,
- you may ship anything other than `ReleaseSafe`,
- or you need defined behavior for **very long sessions** instead of trapping at 255 inputs.

6) **Optional advanced path**

If that threshold is crossed, the next step is not a rewrite of Telex logic; it’s just:
- make wrappers return status instead of trapping,
- replace `u8 buffer_length` with a wider type or explicit saturation/reset,
- and centralize state validation in one internal helper.

For the current review, though, I’d finalize with the narrower conclusion:

> **No obvious current OOB in diacritic production paths, but add explicit assertions around `buffer_length`, `buffer_effective_last`, long-buffer state gating, and C ABI pointer validity before signing off.**

1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Diacritics
8: 
9: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
10: Tones have a separate section.
11: 
12: #### Empty
13: 
14: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
15: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
16:   characters.
17: 
18: #### Circumflex
19: 
20: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
21: 
22: #### Breve
23: 
24: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
25: 
26: #### Horn
27: 
28: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
29: 
30: #### Stroke
31: 
32: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
33: 
34: #### Apply rules
35: 
36: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
37: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
38:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
39:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
40:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
41: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
42:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
43:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
44:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
45: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
46: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
47: 
48: #### Override rules
49: 
50: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
51:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
52:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
53: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
54: - Keep tone untouched.
55: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
56: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
57: 
58: #### Cancel rules
59: 
60: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
61:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
62: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
63:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
64:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
65:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
66:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
67: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
68: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
69:   remains until the new word.
70:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
71: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
72: 
73: #### Auto-fill rules
74: 
75: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
76:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
77: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
78:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
79:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
80:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
81: - The new input character is appended literally.
82: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
83: 
84: ### Tones
85: 
86: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
87: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
88: These vowel could take any tone.
89: 
90: #### Level (ngang)
91: 
92: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
93:   corresponding variants.
94: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
95: 
96: #### Rising (sắc)
97: 
98: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
99: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
100:   -> `Ớ`.
101: 
102: #### Falling (huyền)
103: 
104: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
105: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
106:   -> `Ờ`.
107: 
108: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
109: 
110: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
112:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
113: 
114: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
115: 
116: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
118:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
119: 
120: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
121: 
122: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
124:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
125: 
126: #### Apply rules
127: 
128: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
129:   character literally.
130: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
131:   their lower cases.
132: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
133: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
134: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
135:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
136: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
137: 
138: #### Cancel rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
147:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
148: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
149:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
150: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
151: 
152: #### Reset rules
153: 
154: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
155:   character literally.
156: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
157: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
159:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
160: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
161:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
162: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
163:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
164: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
165: 
166: #### Override rules
167: 
168: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
169:   character literally.
170: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
171:   their lower cases.
172: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
173: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
174: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
175:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
176:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
177: 
178: #### Scan pseudo-word
179: 
180: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
181:   the tone in the correct position.
182: - The existing characters must not be empty.
183: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
184: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
185:   character to scan, then stop.
186: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
187:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
188: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
189:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
190:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
191: 
192: #### Tone position rules
193: 
194: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
195: 
196: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
197:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
198: - Exceptions:
199:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
200: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
201: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
202:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
203:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
204:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
205:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
206:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
207:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
208:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
209:     consonants, put on second vowel.
210:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
211: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
212: - Could not put tone for other cases.

### Review Focus and Context
The thread centers on a comprehensive review of the production code in `src/liblex.zig` to identify memory safety issues and define strict invariant assertions. The engine uses a fixed-size buffer for Vietnamese input processing and exposes a C ABI for FFI (Foreign Function Interface) integration.

### Key Data Structures and Invariants

**State Struct Definition (`src/liblex.zig`):**
```zig
const State = struct {
    // The effective buffer to process Vietnamese input. we will skip processing if the buffer
    // longer than 16.
    buffer_effective: [16]Span,
    // The maximum buffer length that the engine still keeps the effective buffer, after the maximum
    // value (255), we will reset the effective buffer and this value.
    buffer_length: u8 = 0,
    // Mark the earliest position (inclusive) in buffer where we modified the span...
    buffer_modification_index: ?u8 = null,
    // Mark the position (inclusive) in buffer where we will switch to literal mode...
    literal_index: ?u8 = null,
    mode: InputMode = .literal,
};
```

**Span Struct Invariants:**
- `Span.init_diacritic_tone` (lines 41-83) uses `assert(std.ascii.isAlphabetic(base))` and `unreachable` in switch statements to enforce valid Vietnamese character combinations (e.g., only 'A', 'E', 'O' can have a circumflex).

### Memory Safety Concerns and Observations

1.  **Buffer Indexing Safety:**
    - The assistant noted that while `buffer_effective` is fixed at 16, `buffer_length` is a `u8` (max 255). 
    - **Finding:** "The diacritic implementation keeps the buffer as a fixed `[16]Span` and mostly avoids writes after index 15 by switching to literal mode, but the long-input bookkeeping path needs closer attention because `buffer_length` is `u8`."

2.  **`State.add` and `append_literal` Logic:**
    - `append_literal` (lines 731-747) checks `self.buffer_length < self.buffer_effective.len` before writing to the array, but increments `buffer_length` regardless. 
    - It sets `literal_index` to `buffer_effective.len` (16) when `buffer_length` reaches 17, effectively disabling Telex processing for subsequent characters.

3.  **Existing Assertions in `State.add` (lines 249-272):**
    - `assert(std.ascii.isAlphabetic(c));` (Input validation).
    - `assert(self.buffer_modification_index == null or self.buffer_modification_index.? < self.buffer_effective.len);` (Bounds check).
    - `assert(literal_index <= self.buffer_effective.len);` (Literal index must not exceed buffer capacity).
    - `assert(literal_index < self.buffer_length);` (Literal index must be within the total input length).

4.  **C ABI Wrappers and Alignment:**
    - `lex_state_init` (lines 1665-1671) includes an alignment check: `assert(@intFromPtr(state) % @alignOf(State) == 0);`.
    - The engine exports `lex_state_size` and `lex_state_alignment` to allow callers to allocate memory correctly.

### Review Task for Oracle Tool
The assistant requested a second opinion on:
- **Memory-safety risks:** Specifically in `State.add`, `append_literal`, and `buffer_effective` indexing.
- **Missing invariants:** Focus on C ABI wrappers and `assert` placement.
- **Context:** Production builds use `ReleaseSafe`, and the engine must handle long-running input safely despite the `u8` length limit.

progress.message:
1) **TL;DR**

I do **not** see an immediate out-of-bounds bug in the current diacritic-rule paths under the intended invariants and `ReleaseSafe`: writes to `buffer_effective` are guarded, and the `-1`/`-2` reads are mostly preceded by length checks or `literal_index` gating.  
The review report should, however, call out **four concrete invariant gaps**: missing `u8` overflow guard in `append_literal`, incomplete precondition in `buffer_effective_last`, missing “long buffer implies no more effective-buffer reads” invariant in `State.add`, and incomplete C ABI validation in `lex_add` / `lex_backspace` (plus `lex_state_init` not rejecting null explicitly).

2) **Recommended approach (simple path, S <1h)**

Report these findings and suggest adding these assertions only:

1. **`append_literal` needs an explicit `buffer_length` overflow guard**  
   File: `src/liblex.zig:731-747`

   High-confidence issue:
   - Comment says `buffer_length` resets after 255 (`src/liblex.zig:228-230`), but that reset is **not implemented**.
   - `append_literal` always does `self.buffer_length += 1;`.
   - In `ReleaseSafe`, this becomes a runtime trap after 255 inputs. That is not an OOB write, but it is still a production-path failure.

   Suggested assertion:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_length < std.math.maxInt(u8));
   ```
   Place it immediately before `self.buffer_length += 1`.

   Optional related invariant at entry:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len or self.mode == .literal or self.literal_index != null);
   ```

2. **`buffer_effective_last` should assert `buffer_length > 0` itself**  
   File: `src/liblex.zig:749-755`

   Current code only asserts:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len);
   ```
   but then indexes:
   ```zig
   self.buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 1]
   ```
   So the helper’s real contract is “`1 <= buffer_length <= len`”, not just “`buffer_length <= len`”.

   Suggested assertion:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_length > 0);
   assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len);
   ```

   This is important because it moves the safety contract into the helper that does the indexing, instead of relying entirely on callers.

3. **`State.add` is missing the converse long-buffer invariant**  
   File: `src/liblex.zig:249-272`

   Today `add` checks that `literal_index`, if present, is sane. What it does **not** assert is the safety-critical converse:

   > if `buffer_length` is already beyond `[16]Span`, then the state must be in a mode that guarantees we never read `buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 1]` or `-2`.

   Suggested assertion near the top of `add`:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len or self.mode == .literal or self.literal_index != null);
   ```

   Why this matters:
   - Current logic is safe because once Telex overflows the effective buffer, `literal_index` is set and subsequent calls short-circuit into literal append paths.
   - But that safety currently exists as an emergent property, not as an explicit invariant.
   - This assertion would catch future regressions or corrupted state before any `buffer_effective_last()` / `self.buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 2]` access.

4. **C ABI wrappers should explicitly reject null and consistently assert alignment**  
   Files: `src/liblex.zig:1665-1670`, `1718-1725`; header `src/liblex.h:17-25`

   Concrete findings:
   - `lex_state_init` checks alignment, but **null passes that check** because `0 % align == 0`.
   - `lex_add` and `lex_backspace` do **not** mirror the alignment assert at all.
   - All three wrappers then cast and dereference the pointer.

   Suggested assertions in **all** three wrappers:
   ```zig
   assert(@intFromPtr(state) != 0);
   assert(@intFromPtr(state) % @alignOf(State) == 0);
   ```

   Notes for the report:
   - This does not let you verify allocation **size** from a raw pointer; `lex_state_size`/header docs remain the only size contract.
   - But it does make the ABI contract much clearer and catches bad C callers earlier.

5. **Tighten `buffer_modification_index` to the live prefix, not just array bounds**  
   File: `src/liblex.zig:253-255`

   Current assert:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_modification_index == null or self.buffer_modification_index.? < self.buffer_effective.len);
   ```

   Better invariant:
   ```zig
   assert(self.buffer_modification_index == null or
       (self.buffer_modification_index.? < self.buffer_effective.len and
        self.buffer_modification_index.? < self.buffer_length));
   ```

   This is more of a correctness / future-proofing guard than a current memory-safety bug, but it is worth mentioning because the field represents a position in the **live** buffer, not just the allocated array.

3) **Rationale and trade-offs**

- The current diacritic paths are **mostly disciplined**:
  - `append_literal` only writes when `buffer_length < 16`.
  - Reads at `buffer_length - 1` and `buffer_length - 2` are generally gated by `buffer_length == 0` / `< 2` checks or by `literal_index != null`.
  - That means I would **not** report an existing clear OOB read/write in the diacritic branches themselves.

- The real problem is that some of the safety assumptions are only encoded indirectly:
  - helper preconditions are incomplete,
  - long-input behavior depends on a non-obvious invariant,
  - and the C ABI assumes well-behaved callers more than the rest of the code does.

- I would keep the report focused on **assert strengthening**, not redesign. A rewrite is unnecessary right now.

4) **Risks and guardrails**

- **Biggest real production risk today:** long-running input eventually hits `u8` overflow and traps in `ReleaseSafe`.  
  That is an availability issue, not an OOB write.

- **FFI risk:** null / misaligned C pointers are not defended consistently.  
  In particular, `lex_state_init`’s current alignment assert does **not** reject null.

- **Guardrail on wording:** because you’re targeting `ReleaseSafe`, I’d avoid claiming “memory corruption” unless you verify a `ReleaseFast` build too. The safer wording is:
  - “no immediate OOB found under intended invariants,”
  - “but several invariants are missing or only implicit,”
  - “and one long-input trap path is real.”

5) **When to consider the advanced path**

Consider a more invasive hardening pass only if one of these becomes true:

- you want the C ABI to survive **hostile/untrusted callers** instead of trapping,
- you may ship anything other than `ReleaseSafe`,
- or you need defined behavior for **very long sessions** instead of trapping at 255 inputs.

6) **Optional advanced path**

If that threshold is crossed, the next step is not a rewrite of Telex logic; it’s just:
- make wrappers return status instead of trapping,
- replace `u8 buffer_length` with a wider type or explicit saturation/reset,
- and centralize state validation in one internal helper.

For the current review, though, I’d finalize with the narrower conclusion:

> **No obvious current OOB in diacritic production paths, but add explicit assertions around `buffer_length`, `buffer_effective_last`, long-buffer state gating, and C ABI pointer validity before signing off.**

content.text:
## Review result

I reviewed `src/liblex.zig` diacritic handling, especially `State.add`, `append_literal`, `buffer_effective_last`, auto-fill branches, and the C ABI wrappers.

Current covered paths pass:

```sh
zig test src/liblex.zig
# All 23 tests passed.
```

Main conclusion: I do **not** see a current out-of-bounds read/write in the normal diacritic paths when the existing state invariants hold and the code is built in `ReleaseSafe`. Writes to `buffer_effective` are guarded, and most `buffer_length - 1` / `buffer_length - 2` reads are length-gated.

However, several memory-safety invariants are implicit or incomplete.

## Findings

### 1. `buffer_length: u8` can overflow after 255 appends

[`buffer_length` is `u8`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L224-L240), but [`append_literal` increments it unconditionally](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L730-L747):

```zig
self.buffer_length += 1;
```

The comment says the state resets after max value 255, but that reset is not implemented. In `ReleaseSafe`, Zig traps on integer overflow. In unsafe build modes, this becomes unchecked illegal behavior.

Suggested invariant assert before increment:

```zig
assert(self.buffer_length < std.math.maxInt(u8));
self.buffer_length += 1;
```

Better production fix later: either implement the documented reset behavior or widen `buffer_length` to `usize`/`u16`.

---

### 2. Long-buffer safety depends on an implicit invariant

After `buffer_length` exceeds `buffer_effective.len`, Telex mode relies on `literal_index != null` to avoid reading outside `[16]Span`. That invariant is not asserted at the top of [`State.add`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L249-L272).

Suggested assert near the top of `State.add`, after `mode` validation:

```zig
assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len or
    self.mode == .literal or
    self.literal_index != null);
```

This protects direct indexing branches such as the auto-fill rules, which read:

```zig
self.buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 2]
self.buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 1]
```

Examples: [`C/c` auto-fill branch](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L315-L344), repeated for `I/M/N/P/T/U`.

---

### 3. `buffer_effective_last` has an incomplete precondition

[`buffer_effective_last`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L749-L755) asserts only:

```zig
assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len);
```

But it indexes:

```zig
return self.buffer_effective[self.buffer_length - 1];
```

Its real contract is:

```zig
assert(self.buffer_length > 0);
assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len);
```

Current call sites mostly check `buffer_length == 0` first, but the helper should enforce its own indexing invariant.

---

### 4. `buffer_modification_index` should be constrained to the live prefix

Current assert in [`State.add`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L253-L255):

```zig
assert(self.buffer_modification_index == null or
    self.buffer_modification_index.? < self.buffer_effective.len);
```

This only proves the index fits the physical array. It should also prove the index refers to an existing logical span.

Suggested stricter invariant:

```zig
assert(self.buffer_modification_index == null or
    (self.buffer_modification_index.? < self.buffer_effective.len and
     self.buffer_modification_index.? < self.buffer_length));
```

This matters because `buffer_modification_index` is later used to calculate synthetic backspaces/replacements.

---

### 5. C ABI pointer validation is incomplete

[`lex_state_init`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L1665-L1670) checks alignment:

```zig
assert(@intFromPtr(state) % @alignOf(State) == 0);
```

But null pointer address `0` passes that alignment check. [`lex_add` and `lex_backspace`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L1716-L1726) do not explicitly assert null/alignment before casting.

Suggested asserts in all three ABI wrappers:

```zig
assert(@intFromPtr(state) != 0);
assert(@intFromPtr(state) % @alignOf(State) == 0);
```

This does not prove allocation size; the size contract remains documented in [`liblex.h`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.h#L11-L25).

---

### 6. Span comparison helpers should assert valid input bases

[`equals_ignore_case_and_tone`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L85-L90) asserts the argument base is alphabetic, but not `self.base`.

[`equals_base`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L93-L98) has a comment but no assert.

Suggested asserts:

```zig
assert(std.ascii.isAlphabetic(self.base));
assert(std.ascii.isAlphabetic(base));
```

For stricter safety, add a small `Span.assert_valid()` helper and call it before comparisons and over the active buffer prefix.

---

## Suggested invariant helper

A central state validator would reduce scattered checks:

```zig
fn assert_valid(self: *const State) void {
    assert(self.mode == .literal or self.mode == .telex);

    if (self.mode == .literal) {
        assert(self.literal_index == null);
    }

    assert(self.buffer_length <= self.buffer_effective.len or
        self.mode == .literal or
        self.literal_index != null);

    if (self.literal_index) |literal_index| {
        assert(self.mode == .telex);
        assert(literal_index <= self.buffer_effective.len);
        assert(literal_index < self.buffer_length);
    }

    if (self.buffer_modification_index) |index| {
        assert(index < self.buffer_effective.len);
        assert(index < self.buffer_length);
    }

    const effective_len = @min(self.buffer_length, self.buffer_effective.len);
    for (self.buffer_effective[0..effective_len]) |span| {
        span.assert_valid();
    }
}
```

And on `Span`:

```zig
fn assert_valid(self: Span) void {
    _ = Span.init_diacritic_tone(self.base, self.diacritic, self.tone);
}
```

Use `State.assert_valid()` at least at the start and end of `State.add` and `State.backspace`.

## Secondary non-memory issue

Tone-trigger branches `F/J/R/S/X/Z` currently do nothing in [`State.add`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L413-L715). That can leave `buffer_modification_index` stale from the previous operation. It is not a diacritic memory issue, but it violates the local invariant that every `State.add` action decides `buffer_modification_index`.

Minimal safety pattern:

```zig
self.buffer_modification_index = null;
```

at the start of `State.add`, then mutation branches overwrite it when needed.
